output:
**Alex → Engineer | Donnie → Deckhand | Emily → Neither role (hospitality/people-facing duties)**

**Reasoning:**

- **Alex as Engineer:** Naval Engineering degree, solves complex technical problems on the yacht for fun, strong technical foundation. He's the only crew member qualified for engineering work.

- **Donnie as Deckhand:** Years of deckhand experience on other ships, skilled at rigging, safety procedures, and maintenance — the core deckhand tasks. His mechanical background supports this role well.

- **Emily excluded from both roles:** She struggles with complex technical diagrams (rules out engineer), and while she can do basic deckhand tasks, she lacks enthusiasm and explicitly prefers people/hospitality work. Assigning her to either role would be forcing a poor fit when two better-qualified candidates exist.

**Why this is the only sensible allocation:**

The interpersonal friction (Emily's grudge against Alex, her irritation with Donnie's hygiene) is irrelevant to the assignment. Skills and qualifications dictate the answer — Alex is the only qualified engineer, Donnie is the only experienced deckhand. Swapping them would put a deckhand-experienced mechanic in an engineering role he's less suited for, and waste Alex's engineering degree on deckhand work he's overqualified for.
